## [00:00 - 03:03]

Okay, so we're going to talk a little bit about positioning and choosing a niche. Now when I say niche in this context, we're going to be first and foremost looking at a core competence. So a specific service offer or maybe you could call it a specific process that you have in your business. Don't worry if that sounds complicated. I'm going to explain that in just a moment. When we talk about niching, we also are talking about a specific type of industry or customer avatar. For example, some people might say, I am specializing in e-commerce or my niche is realtors or my niche is gymnastic gyms, etc., etc. First and foremost, I would encourage you to let's look at what are you actually really strong at as a service or as a competence that people are willing to pay for in the market. So we want to make sure that we're not saying, well, I'm really good at tying braids in people's hair and you're trying to be an agency or offering digital marketing services is not going to work. So we want to make sure that we have a competence or a solution that we're really good at that we can replicate across different clients and start with that. And if we can connect that or tie that to a core human desire, which is wealth, health, and love. And so in most cases, you're going to be involved in wealth here. So tying that service to something that people are willing to pay for to solve, basically. As closely as you can connect the two, and I'm going to show you how to do that through your messaging exercises, the better off you will be. This will put you apart from having to compete with people that are charging, you know, $100 or $50 on the market for the same exact service as what you're offering. We want to get away from that and we want to position ourselves in a manner where we can charge the prices where we're not hitting burnout all the time in our business and we're able to sustain success for ourselves and for our clients as well. Starts with niching. So first we look at a core competence and then what we do is we identify the type of people that it will most likely help. Who are the people that that solution can help? Those could be industries or specific personas. You can run a rapid fire test at this stage if you have lots of competencies or lots of different niches that you're thinking about going after. And again, I walk you through exactly how to do that so you can assess which one shows the most potential to go after. One of the things that I want to stress when we're having this conversation is to remind

## [03:03 - 04:31]

you that when you're picking a niche and when you're going to talk to a specific type of avatar, it can feel a little bit restricting to some entrepreneurs because some of you may be saying, well, I can help a lot of people and I've helped these kinds of people and these kinds of people and these kinds of people and I don't want to pick and I don't feel like I can pick. And so it's important to realize that niching is just a process exercise. So you pick a niche to build the process to service that particular avatar as best as possible. Once that process is locked down, you can then go and build out more webs and weave out more niches inside of your business. So it's not to say that you'll only ever work with this particular type of client ever again in your life or as a business. It's to say that you start off with the one that shows the most potential first, the one that will help you to get the most momentum going in your business, get that one nailed down, get it to a point where you know how to get them results on autopilot almost and then build out the other niches from there. So below there are some exercises that you can work through to help you identify your niche and pick which one to go after. But just remember, it is always better to speak to a specific person than to try to be everything to everybody at once, even though you might think you are at the stage.
